question: How is the NuScale seismic design described in Ch.01, and what implications does this have for RCS components per Ch.05?

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The section describes the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) of the NuScale Power Module, highlighting its role in removing noncondensible gases and maintaining the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary. The RCS, located inside the containment vessel, circulates coolant by natural convection to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generators during normal operation, and interfaces with several other safety and control systems.

nuscale_ch01_0747.title: SRP 15.4.6, Rev 2: Inadvertent Decrease in Boron Concentration in the Reactor Coolant System (PWR)II.1 Reactor Coolant and Main Steam System Pressures
nuscale_ch01_0747.summary: This section reviews NuScale’s conformance with SRP 15.4.6, Rev 2, regarding inadvertent decreases in boron concentration in the reactor coolant system for PWRs. It concludes that NuScale’s design meets the relevant regulatory criteria for reactor coolant and main steam system pressures, fuel cladding integrity, incident frequency, and analysis methods, while noting that certain requirements (such as operator action and non-safety systems) are not applicable due to NuScale’s unique design features.

nuscale_ch01_0338.title: SRP 9.2.7, Rev 0: Chilled Water SystemII.1 Quality Standards and Records
nuscale_ch01_0338.summary: The section explains that the NuScale Chilled Water System (CHWS) does not perform safety or containment isolation functions, so quality standards and records requirements are not applicable. Additionally, the CHWS is not classified as Seismic Category I, but it complies with regulatory guidance to ensure its failure would not adversely affect safety-related systems.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation during normal and off-normal conditions.

nuscale_ch01_0748.title: SRP 15.4.6, Rev 2: Inadvertent Decrease in Boron Concentration in the Reactor Coolant System (PWR)II.2 Fuel Cladding Integrity
nuscale_ch01_0748.summary: The section explains that NuScale’s reactor design conforms to the regulatory criteria for fuel cladding integrity in the event of an inadvertent decrease in boron concentration, as outlined in SRP 15.4.6, Rev 2. It notes that "CHF" (Critical Heat Flux) is the more appropriate term for describing the relevant phenomenon in the NuScale context, and confirms that the design meets the required standards for this scenario.

nuscale_ch01_0339.title: SRP 9.2.7, Rev 0: Chilled Water SystemII.2 Protection Against Natural Phenomena
nuscale_ch01_0339.summary: The NuScale chilled water system (CHWS) is not classified as Seismic Category I and does not perform safety or containment isolation functions. While the CHWS is not safety-related, it conforms to regulatory guidance by ensuring that its failure would not adversely impact any safety-related structures, systems, or components (SSCs) during natural phenomena.

nuscale_ch01_0332.title: SRP 9.2.5, Rev 3: Ultimate Heat SinkII.5 Cooling Water System Testing
nuscale_ch01_0332.summary: The section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with SRP 9.2.5, Rev 3 requirements for the Ultimate Heat Sink, specifically noting that the cooling water system testing criteria are fully met. It states that there are no exceptions or comments regarding compliance for cooling water system testing.

nuscale_ch01_0178.title: DSRS 6.6, Rev. 0: Inservice Inspection and Testing of Class 2 and 3 ComponentsII.7 Structural Supports
nuscale_ch01_0178.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for inservice inspection and testing of Class 2 and 3 components, specifically structural supports, as outlined in DSRS 6.6, Rev. 0. It confirms that NuScale meets all relevant requirements, with no exceptions noted, and clarifies that certain SRP sections are not applicable due to differences in reactor design.

nuscale_ch01_0632.title: SRP 14.3.2, Rev 0: Structural and Systems Engineering - Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance CriteriaAll Various
nuscale_ch01_0632.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory criteria related to inspections, tests, analyses, and acceptance criteria (ITAAC) for structural and systems engineering. It details which regulatory requirements are fully or partially met, and notes when certain sections are not applicable, often because the methodology is addressed elsewhere or the criteria pertain only to specific applicants or amendment types.

nuscale_ch01_0417.title: SRP 10.2, Rev 3: Turbine GeneratorII.3 Prevention of Adverse Effects on Safety-Related SSC in the Turbine Building
nuscale_ch01_0417.summary: This section states that the NuScale plant design uses physical barriers to protect safety-related structures, systems, and components (SSCs) from turbine missile effects, making certain regulatory requirements—such as inservice inspection, turbine rotor integrity, and prevention of adverse effects on safety-related SSCs in the turbine building—not applicable. Additionally, there are no safety-related SSCs located in the turbine building, further supporting the inapplicability of these criteria.

nuscale_ch01_0318.title: SRP 9.2.1, Rev 5: Station Service Water SystemII.4 Cooling Water System (GDC 44)
nuscale_ch01_0318.summary: The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report states that the Site Cooling Water System (SCWS) is not safety-related and does not provide essential cooling to safety-related structures, systems, and components (SSC). The SCWS design meets relevant regulatory criteria to ensure its failure does not adversely affect safety-related SSCs, but GDC 44 (Cooling Water System) is not applicable to this system.

nuscale_ch01_0150.title: DSRS 3.11, Rev. 0: Environmental Qualification of Mechanical and Electrical EquipmentII.15 Application of RG 1.100 for Seismic Qualification of Electrical and Active Mechanical Equipment and Functional Qualification of Active Mechanical Equipment for Nuclear Power Plants
nuscale_ch01_0150.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory guidance for the environmental and seismic qualification of mechanical and electrical equipment in nuclear power plants. Specifically, it notes that NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.100 for seismic and functional qualification of electrical and active mechanical equipment, with further details referenced in Table 1.9-2. The section demonstrates NuScale’s approach to meeting safety-related equipment qualification requirements as outlined in DSRS 3.11, Rev. 0.

nuscale_ch01_0334.title: SRP 9.2.6, Rev 3: Condensate Storage FacilitiesII.2 Environmental and Dynamic Effects Design Basis
nuscale_ch01_0334.summary: The NuScale condensate storage system is not safety-related or risk-significant and is located outside the Seismic Category I Reactor Building, so its failure would not impact safety-related systems or plant operation. The design basis for environmental and dynamic effects is met, with no additional requirements, and sharing of the facility does not impair safety functions. There are also no concerns regarding radioactive releases or 10 CFR 20.1406 compliance for this system.

nuscale_ch01_0147.title: DSRS 3.11, Rev. 0: Environmental Qualification of Mechanical and Electrical EquipmentII.12 Application of RG 1.158 for Environmental Qualification of Class 1E Lead Storage Batteries
nuscale_ch01_0147.summary: This section summarizes NuScale's conformance with various regulatory guides (RGs) related to the environmental qualification of mechanical and electrical equipment. Specifically, for RG 1.158 concerning Class 1E lead storage batteries, the requirement is marked as "Not Applicable," with a reference to Table 1.9-2 for further details. Other RGs in this section are noted as either conforming, partially conforming, or not applicable, with cross-references provided.

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: This section describes the materials and design requirements for threaded fasteners used in key reactor coolant system components, emphasizing the use of Alloy 718 for its compatibility and stress corrosion cracking resistance. It also outlines the preservice and inservice inspection and testing procedures for reactor coolant pressure boundary components, specifying adherence to the latest ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) standards as mandated by federal regulations.

nuscale_ch01_0312.title: SRP 9.1.5, Rev 1: Overhead Heavy Load Handling SystemsII.2 Specific Criteria to Meet GDC 2
nuscale_ch01_0312.summary: This section documents NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for overhead heavy load handling systems, specifically addressing compliance with General Design Criterion (GDC) 2. The report confirms that the design meets the required safety standards, with no exceptions or additional comments noted for this criterion.

nuscale_ch01_0307.title: SRP 9.1.4, Rev 4: Light Load Handling System and Refueling Cavity DesignII.1 Specific Criteria to Meet GDC 2
nuscale_ch01_0307.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for the Light Load Handling System and Refueling Cavity Design, specifically addressing compliance with General Design Criteria (GDC) 2, 5, 61, and 62 as outlined in SRP 9.1.4, Rev 4. The document indicates that NuScale’s design meets these criteria, with no exceptions noted, as part of its Final Safety Analysis Report.

nuscale_ch05_0008.title: Compliance with Codes and Code Cases
nuscale_ch05_0008.summary: This section describes how the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) comply with relevant U.S. nuclear regulations, specifically 10 CFR 50.55a and associated General Design Criteria, by designing, fabricating, and testing reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Class 1 standards. It also clarifies the classification of connected piping and references the applicable ASME BPVC edition and regulatory guides used for the design certification.

edges.description: Section A describes specific RCS design features (e.g., degasification line, RVVs) that maintain RCPB integrity, while Section B discusses how NuScale's unique design (absence of reactor coolant pumps/loops) affects the applicability of certain regulatory criteria, connecting the seismic design implications for RCS components by highlighting design differences and regulatory conformance.

Section A describes the RCS components' design and safety functions, while Section B outlines the seismic categorization and regulatory conformance of auxiliary systems, connecting them through the implication that only Seismic Category I systems (like the RCS) are designed to withstand seismic events per regulatory requirements.

Section B provides a general overview of the RCS design and its reliance on natural circulation, while Section A specifies detailed features and operational aspects of the RCS components described in Section B, such as degasification and pressure boundary integrity.

Section A describes specific RCS design features (e.g., degasification line, RVVs) that maintain system integrity, while Section B discusses regulatory criteria for RCS safety analysis and notes that certain criteria are not applicable to NuScale due to its unique design (absence of coolant pumps), thus Section A's design details provide the technical basis for the regulatory exceptions noted in Section B.

Section A describes the RCS system's safety functions and integrity, while Section B specifies that certain support systems (like condensate storage) are not safety-related or Seismic Category I, clarifying which components are subject to seismic design requirements as described in Ch.01 and their implications for RCS components in Ch.05.

Section A discusses the inapplicability of certain PWR-specific seismic and operational requirements to the NuScale design due to the absence of reactor coolant pumps, while Section B clarifies that the condensate storage system is not safety-related and is located outside the Seismic Category I structure, indicating both sections address how seismic categorization and system design choices affect the applicability of regulatory requirements to RCS components.

Section A explains that certain regulatory guidance for PWRs with forced coolant flow and pumps is not applicable to NuScale because its seismic design omits reactor coolant pumps, while Section B describes the NuScale RCS as relying on natural circulation without pumps, thus providing the design basis that justifies the regulatory position in Section A.

Section A and Section B both discuss the inapplicability of certain SRP guidance to the NuScale design due to the absence of reactor coolant pumps and loops, connecting the implications for both reactor coolant system pressures and fuel cladding integrity.

Section A discusses how the NuScale design omits reactor coolant pumps and thus certain seismic-related SRP guidance is not applicable, while Section B clarifies that the condensate storage system is not safety-related and is located outside the Seismic Category I structure, connecting both sections through their treatment of seismic design implications for RCS and related components.

Section A explains that certain support systems like the condensate storage are not safety-related or Seismic Category I, while Section B describes the RCS as a safety-significant system integral to the NuScale module, highlighting that only specific components (like the RCS) are subject to stringent seismic design as described in Ch.01, which has implications for their regulatory treatment.

Section A explains that certain non-safety-related systems are not seismically classified and do not impact safety-related systems, while Section B clarifies that specific regulatory guidance for RCS components is not applicable to NuScale due to its unique design without reactor coolant pumps, connecting both sections through the implications of seismic and safety classifications on RCS component requirements.

Section A and Section B both describe the NuScale condensate storage system as non-safety-related, not risk-significant, and located outside the Seismic Category I building, with no reasonable potential to impact safety-related systems during seismic events, thus providing consistent evidence regarding seismic design implications for RCS components.

Section A describes the NuScale RCS design as relying on natural circulation without pumps or external piping, which Section B references to justify that certain regulatory criteria for forced-flow PWRs do not apply, due to the absence of pumps in the NuScale design.

Section A describes the RCS design as relying on natural circulation without external piping, while Section B discusses the seismic categorization and protection of auxiliary systems, implying that the seismic design approach in Ch.01 affects which RCS components are classified as safety-related and require seismic protection.

Section A discusses the inapplicability of certain PWR-specific seismic and coolant loop requirements to NuScale due to its unique design (no reactor coolant pumps), while Section B addresses the seismic categorization and location of non-safety-related condensate storage systems, both highlighting how NuScale's seismic design choices (Ch.01) affect the classification and protection of RCS-related components (Ch.05).

Section A discusses the NuScale seismic design's conformance with regulatory criteria for protection against natural phenomena, which directly impacts the integrity and operational requirements of RCS components described in Section B, as these components must maintain pressure boundary integrity under seismic events.

Section A discusses the NuScale seismic design's conformance with regulatory criteria for protection against natural phenomena, which is relevant to Section B's analysis of RCS component pressures and system responses under abnormal conditions, as seismic design integrity directly affects RCS safety margins.

Both Section A and Section B discuss how NuScale's water-related systems are designed to protect against natural phenomena, specifically seismic events, and describe the implications for safety-related systems such as the RCS components by ensuring failures do not adversely impact essential SSCs.

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria and inspection/testing requirements for structural supports of Class 2 and 3 components, which are relevant to the seismic design described in Section B's summary of RCS component functions and integrity, thus connecting the seismic design basis to the operational and safety requirements for RCS components.

Section A and Section B both clarify that certain SRP sections are not applicable to the NuScale design due to its unique features (no ice condenser containment and no reactor coolant pumps), connecting the seismic design context in Ch.01 to the implications for RCS components in Ch.05 by exclusion of inapplicable regulatory criteria.

Section A discusses the inapplicability of certain SRP sections to NuScale's design due to the absence of ice condenser containment, while Section B clarifies that the condensate storage system is not safety-related and is located outside the Seismic Category I building, both providing context on how seismic design considerations in Ch.01 affect the classification and regulatory treatment of RCS components in Ch.05.

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria and testing requirements for seismic design verification, while Section B describes the RCS components whose integrity and function must be demonstrated to meet those seismic design criteria.

Section A outlines the inspection, testing, and acceptance criteria for NuScale's seismic design as part of its safety analysis, while Section B discusses how the unique features of the NuScale RCS—specifically the absence of reactor coolant pumps—affect the applicability of certain regulatory requirements, connecting them through the implications of seismic design choices on RCS component behavior and regulatory conformance.

Section A outlines the inspection, testing, and acceptance criteria for seismic design in NuScale (Ch.01), while Section B discusses the seismic categorization and safety implications for RCS-related support systems (Ch.05), connecting them through the regulatory evaluation of how seismic design affects the safety classification and protection of RCS components.

Section A discusses the NuScale design's approach to protecting safety systems from natural phenomena (including seismic events), which directly impacts the requirements for inservice inspection and structural support of RCS components as described in Section B.

Section A discusses the NuScale design's conformance with seismic protection requirements for cooling water systems, while Section B outlines the inspections, tests, and acceptance criteria (including seismic-related tests) that verify such design features, thus Section B provides the verification framework for the seismic design described in Section A.

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria for inservice inspection and testing of structural supports, while Section B details the inspections, tests, analyses, and acceptance criteria that must be met, connecting them through the requirement that seismic design features (from Ch.01) must be verified for RCS components (Ch.05) via these inspection and testing programs.

Section A states that there are no safety-related SSCs in the Turbine Building, meaning seismic design considerations for turbine missiles or adverse effects do not apply, while Section B describes the RCS components and their safety functions, implying that seismic design implications from Ch.01 do not directly affect RCS components per Ch.05.

Both sections discuss the inapplicability of certain standard regulatory criteria to the NuScale design due to its unique features (e.g., lack of safety-related SSCs in the Turbine Building in Section A and absence of reactor coolant pumps in Section B), highlighting how the seismic and system design described in Ch.01 affects the regulatory treatment of RCS components in Ch.05.

Both sections discuss how non-safety-related systems and components (in the Turbine Building and condensate storage) are located outside of Seismic Category I structures and are designed or justified such that their failure does not adversely impact safety-related SSCs, connecting the seismic design approach in Ch.01 to the implications for RCS components in Ch.05.

Section A explains that the site cooling water system is not safety-related but must not adversely affect Seismic Category I SSCs, while Section B describes RCS components that are part of the safety-related reactor coolant pressure boundary, connecting them through the requirement that nonsafety-related systems like SCWS must not compromise the seismic integrity of safety-related RCS components.

Section A explains that the NuScale site cooling water system is not safety-related and only needs to ensure its failure does not adversely affect Seismic Category I SSCs, while Section B clarifies that certain non-safety system requirements are not applicable to NuScale's RCS design due to the absence of reactor coolant pumps, connecting both sections through the implications of seismic and safety classifications on RCS component requirements.

Both Section A and Section B describe how non-safety-related water systems (SCWS and condensate storage) are designed such that their failure does not adversely affect Seismic Category I safety-related SSCs, connecting their seismic design implications for RCS components.

Section A and Section B both state that the NuScale condensate storage system is neither safety-related nor risk-significant and is located outside the Seismic Category I Reactor Building, indicating that its failure does not adversely impact safety-related systems, thus providing consistent evidence regarding seismic design implications for RCS components.

Section A discusses the environmental qualification of safety-related mechanical and electrical equipment per regulatory guidance, while Section B addresses the seismic and safety categorization of the condensate storage system, connecting them through the regulatory evaluation of equipment qualification and seismic categorization as described in the NuScale design.

Both sections clarify that certain systems (turbine building SSCs in Section A and the station service water system in Section B) are not safety-related or important-to-safety, and thus their seismic design or failure does not adversely affect safety-related RCS components, connecting the seismic design implications from Ch.01 to the RCS context in Ch.05.

Section A discusses the absence of safety-related SSCs in the Turbine Building and the use of barriers for protection, while Section B addresses the seismic and environmental qualification requirements for mechanical and electrical equipment, connecting them through the regulatory need to ensure that only qualified, safety-related components (such as RCS components) are subject to seismic design and qualification as described in Ch.01 and Ch.05.

Both sections describe how non-safety-related systems (turbine building SSCs in Section A and condensate storage facilities in Section B) are located and designed such that their failure or dynamic effects, including seismic events, do not adversely impact safety-related systems, thus supporting the overall seismic design approach described in Ch.01 and its implications for RCS components in Ch.05.

Section A describes the exclusion of safety-related SSCs from the Turbine Building based on seismic and missile protection design, while Section B addresses the environmental qualification of safety-related equipment (including seismic considerations) elsewhere, connecting them through the regulatory approach to protecting and qualifying safety-related components under seismic events.

Section A discusses the seismic design considerations for the NuScale site cooling water system, specifically limiting GDC 2 applicability to prevent adverse effects on Seismic Category I SSCs, while Section B addresses the environmental and seismic qualification of mechanical and electrical equipment (including RCS components) per regulatory guidance, connecting them through the shared focus on seismic qualification and protection of safety-related systems.

Both Section A and Section B describe how nonsafety-related water systems (SCWS and condensate storage) are designed such that their failure does not adversely affect Seismic Category I safety-related SSCs, connecting the seismic design approach in Ch.01 to implications for RCS components in Ch.05.
